steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis (as the precipitant of pancreatitis), hepatic pathology (given markedly elevated AST/ALT/ALP), possible malignancy-related process given breast cancer history
ensemble.information_gap: The etiology and anatomic severity of what is almost certainly acute pancreatitis is unknown — specifically whether there is gallstone/biliary obstruction driving it, whether pancreatic necrosis or peripancreatic fluid collections are present, and whether there is a hepatic or biliary ductal abnormality explaining the markedly elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin
ensemble.expected_finding: Pancreatic edema, peripancreatic fat stranding, or necrosis confirming acute pancreatitis and its severity; dilated common bile duct or choledocholithiasis explaining the transaminase/ALP/bilirubin elevation; or an alternative intra-abdominal source of pathology
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 17,740 IU/L confirming severe acute pancreatitis, markedly elevated liver enzymes (AST 407, ALT 465, ALP 467) and elevated bilirubin suggesting a biliary etiology, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to assess pancreatic severity, identify complications, and evaluate the biliary tree.
grounding: pain was described as radiating around both sides and into her back, worse on the L than on the R
severe abdominal pain in a band-like distribution in her epigastric region underneath both her breasts
PMH: breast ca, s/p lumpectomy and radiation
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed severe acute pancreatitis (lipase ~296× upper limit of normal) with a classic band-like epigastric pain radiating to the back, but the markedly elevated transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and direct bilirubin strongly suggest a biliary etiology (choledocholithiasis) that requires anatomic characterization. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast will allow me to grade pancreatitis severity (necrosis, fluid collections), identify biliary ductal dilation or stones, and — given her breast cancer history — exclude any hepatic metastatic process contributing to the liver enzyme abnormalities.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: biliary obstruction/choledocholithiasis (as the precipitant of pancreatitis), hepatic pathology (given markedly elevated AST/ALT/ALP), possible malignancy-related process given breast cancer history
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The etiology and anatomic severity of what is almost certainly acute pancreatitis is unknown — specifically whether there is gallstone/biliary obstruction driving it, whether pancreatic necrosis or peripancreatic fluid collections are present, and whether there is a hepatic or biliary ductal abnormality explaining the markedly elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin

representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: With a lipase of 17,740 IU/L confirming severe acute pancreatitis, markedly elevated liver enzymes (AST 407, ALT 465, ALP 467) and elevated bilirubin suggesting a biliary etiology, CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast is the appropriate next step to assess pancreatic severity, identify complications, and evaluate the biliary tree.
grounding: pain was described as radiating around both sides and into her back, worse on the L than on the R
severe abdominal pain in a band-like distribution in her epigastric region underneath both her breasts
PMH: breast ca, s/p lumpectomy and radiation
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has biochemically confirmed severe acute pancreatitis (lipase ~296× upper limit of normal) with a classic band-like epigastric pain radiating to the back, but the markedly elevated transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and direct bilirubin strongly suggest a biliary etiology (choledocholithiasis) that requires anatomic characterization. CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast will allow me to grade pancreatitis severity (necrosis, fluid collections), identify biliary ductal dilation or stones, and — given her breast cancer history — exclude any hepatic metastatic process contributing to the liver enzyme abnormalities.

vindication.actual_finding: CT showed peripancreatic fat stranding and fluid surrounding the head, body, and tail of the pancreas with normal parenchymal enhancement, consistent with acute pancreatitis, but with normal gallbladder, bile ducts, and liver, and no choledocholithiasis or biliary dilation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The scan confirmed acute pancreatitis with peripancreatic stranding/fluid and no necrosis (partially matching the expected finding), but did not identify a biliary or hepatic etiology to explain the elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin, leaving that portion of the information gap unresolved.

steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis
ensemble.information_gap: The CT confirmed pancreatitis but showed a normal-appearing gallbladder and bile ducts without identifying gallstones or biliary dilation; the etiology of the pancreatitis (gallstone/biliary vs. other) remains undetermined, and ultrasound is the superior modality for detecting cholelithiasis and subtle biliary pathology.
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallstones (cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis), gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis, or common bile duct dilation indicating biliary obstruction as the precipitant of pancreatitis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: With markedly elevated lipase (17740), elevated transaminases (AST 407, ALT 465), elevated alkaline phosphatase (467), and elevated total bilirubin (1.8) confirming acute pancreatitis with a hepatobiliary pattern, ultrasound is the standard first-line test to identify gallstones or biliary obstruction as the causative etiology, which CT with contrast is known to miss.
grounding: CT ABD FINDINGS: stranding and fluid seen surrounding the head, body, and tail of the pancreas, which appear to enhance normally, consistent with pancreatitis
CT ABD FINDINGS: The gallbladder, intra- and extra-hepatic bile ducts, and spleen are normal
CT ABD FINDINGS: The pancreatic duct is prominent, although not enlarged
ensemble.reasoning: The CT has confirmed acute pancreatitis and the markedly elevated transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin point strongly toward a biliary/gallstone etiology, yet the CT did not identify gallstones (which CT routinely misses) or frank biliary dilation. A dedicated right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the gold-standard modality to detect cholelithiasis, microlithiasis, and common bile duct dilation, directly addressing the critical management question of whether this is gallstone pancreatitis requiring early ERCP or cholecystectomy planning.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: The CT confirmed pancreatitis but showed a normal-appearing gallbladder and bile ducts without identifying gallstones or biliary dilation; the etiology of the pancreatitis (gallstone/biliary vs. other) remains undetermined, and ultrasound is the superior modality for detecting cholelithiasis and subtle biliary pathology.
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Gallstones (cholelithiasis or choledocholithiasis), gallbladder wall thickening/pericholecystic fluid suggesting cholecystitis, or common bile duct dilation indicating biliary obstruction as the precipitant of pancreatitis
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: With markedly elevated lipase (17740), elevated transaminases (AST 407, ALT 465), elevated alkaline phosphatase (467), and elevated total bilirubin (1.8) confirming acute pancreatitis with a hepatobiliary pattern, ultrasound is the standard first-line test to identify gallstones or biliary obstruction as the causative etiology, which CT with contrast is known to miss.
grounding: CT ABD FINDINGS: stranding and fluid seen surrounding the head, body, and tail of the pancreas, which appear to enhance normally, consistent with pancreatitis
CT ABD FINDINGS: The gallbladder, intra- and extra-hepatic bile ducts, and spleen are normal
CT ABD FINDINGS: The pancreatic duct is prominent, although not enlarged
representative_ex_ante.reasoning: The CT has confirmed acute pancreatitis and the markedly elevated transaminases, alkaline phosphatase, and bilirubin point strongly toward a biliary/gallstone etiology, yet the CT did not identify gallstones (which CT routinely misses) or frank biliary dilation. A dedicated right-upper-quadrant ultrasound is the gold-standard modality to detect cholelithiasis, microlithiasis, and common bile duct dilation, directly addressing the critical management question of whether this is gallstone pancreatitis requiring early ERCP or cholecystectomy planning.

vindication.actual_finding: Ultrasound demonstrated multiple mobile gallstones plus a larger 17 mm immobile stone in the phrygian cap, with a 6 mm CBD tapering smoothly and no gallbladder wall thickening or biliary dilation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The ultrasound identified cholelithiasis (the primary expected finding) as a likely precipitant of pancreatitis, directly resolving the etiology gap that CT had left open, though no cholecystitis or biliary obstruction was present.

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis; also concern for malignant biliary obstruction given history of breast cancer and incidental adrenal nodule
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is a common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) or other biliary obstruction causing the pancreatitis — the CT showed a prominent pancreatic duct and the ultrasound demonstrated gallstones including a large immobile 17 mm stone, but the CBD was only 6 mm and the distal CBD/ampullary region was not fully characterized; the etiology of the pancreatitis (biliary vs. other) remains unconfirmed
ensemble.expected_finding: A filling defect or stone within the common bile duct or at the ampulla of Vater, or alternatively a stricture/mass at the biliary-pancreatic junction that would explain the markedly elevated transaminases (AST 407, ALT 465), elevated alkaline phosphatase (467), elevated bilirubin (total 1.8, direct 0.9), and massively elevated lipase (17740) in the setting of known gallstones
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive next step to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology given confirmed gallstones on ultrasound, a prominent pancreatic duct on CT, markedly elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin suggesting biliary involvement, and the need to determine whether ERCP is indicated — all without additional radiation or contrast.
grounding: Lipase: 17740.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0) — massively elevated, confirming acute pancreatitis
AST: 407.0 IU/L, ALT: 465.0 IU/L — markedly elevated transaminases suggesting biliary/hepatic involvement
Alkaline Phosphatase: 467.0 IU/L (ref 35.0-105.0) — markedly elevated, consistent with biliary obstruction
Bilirubin, Total: 1.8 mg/dL, Direct: 0.9 mg/dL — elevated direct bilirubin suggesting biliary pathology
CT ABD: 'stranding and fluid seen surrounding the head, body, and tail of the pancreas... The pancreatic duct is prominent, although not enlarged'
US: 'There are multiple mobile gallstones with a larger, 17 mm stone which is immobile and located within the phrygian cap'
US: 'The CBD measures 6 mm, tapering smoothly as it nears the papilla' — CBD not dilated but distal segment not fully characterized
CT ABD: 'incompletely characterized 1 cm nodule of the left adrenal gland. The right adrenal gland is somewhat thickened' — raises concern for metastatic disease given breast cancer history
ensemble.reasoning: The clinical and laboratory picture is consistent with acute pancreatitis (lipase 17740) with a biliary pattern of liver injury (AST 407, ALT 465, ALP 467, direct bilirubin 0.9), and ultrasound has already confirmed gallstones. However, the distal CBD and ampullary region were not fully characterized on ultrasound, and while the CBD measured 6 mm, a passed or impacted stone at the ampulla cannot be excluded. MRCP is ordered to non-invasively delineate the biliary and pancreatic ductal anatomy, specifically to identify choledocholithiasis or ampullary obstruction that would mandate ERCP, and to evaluate the prominent pancreatic duct seen on CT for any underlying ductal pathology.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction as the etiology of pancreatitis; also concern for malignant biliary obstruction given history of breast cancer and incidental adrenal nodule
representative_ex_ante.information_gap: Whether there is a common bile duct stone (choledocholithiasis) or other biliary obstruction causing the pancreatitis — the CT showed a prominent pancreatic duct and the ultrasound demonstrated gallstones including a large immobile 17 mm stone, but the CBD was only 6 mm and the distal CBD/ampullary region was not fully characterized; the etiology of the pancreatitis (biliary vs. other) remains unconfirmed
representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: A filling defect or stone within the common bile duct or at the ampulla of Vater, or alternatively a stricture/mass at the biliary-pancreatic junction that would explain the markedly elevated transaminases (AST 407, ALT 465), elevated alkaline phosphatase (467), elevated bilirubin (total 1.8, direct 0.9), and massively elevated lipase (17740) in the setting of known gallstones
representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: MRCP is the appropriate non-invasive next step to evaluate the biliary tree for choledocholithiasis or ductal pathology given confirmed gallstones on ultrasound, a prominent pancreatic duct on CT, markedly elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin suggesting biliary involvement, and the need to determine whether ERCP is indicated — all without additional radiation or contrast.

US: 'There are multiple mobile gallstones with a larger, 17 mm stone which is immobile and located within the phrygian cap'
US: 'The CBD measures 6 mm, tapering smoothly as it nears the papilla' — CBD not dilated but distal segment not fully characterized

vindication.actual_finding: MRCP showed no choledocholithiasis, a normal-caliber common bile duct (5 mm), no biliary ductal dilatation, and no filling defect or mass at the ampulla, with pancreatitis changes confined to the pancreatic tail.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The expected finding of a CBD stone, ampullary filling defect, or biliary obstruction explaining the elevated liver enzymes and lipase was not present; the biliary system was entirely normal on MRCP.
